articles.description: On 1 July, an Airbus A330 lifts off from Harare for London under the

articles.title: Zimbabwe's tobacco anchor in Britain holds at £129m
articles.description: Of every £100 of goods Zimbabwe sells to Britain, £96 is tobacco or beverages. While the wider UK–Zimbabwe trade relationship softened in the year to December 2025, Zimbabwean tobacco held its grip, and the IMF projects Zimbabwean GDP growth of 7.5% in 2025 and 5.0% in 2026. The product that built Zimbabwe's name as an exporter is still doing the work.

articles.title: The Apple of haircare gets its German cheque
articles.description: Ruka, the London-based textured-hair company founded by Zimbabwean entrepreneur Varaidzo Tendai Moyo and her co-founder Ugorji Agbai, has closed a $4.5m round co-led by Henkel Ventures and Freedom Trail Capital. The deal, which takes lifetime funding to roughly $10m and brings one of the world's largest consumer-goods groups onto the cap table, is the most meaningful endorsement yet of a Zimbabwean diaspora-founded brand.

more.title: Pardon Mapondera: gone but not forgotten at Venice
more.description: The fifth and final piece in our series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Pardon Mapondera, the artist whose practice the room finally arranges itself around, treats discarded plastic and glass jars not as ecological signal but as ritual matter, and produces, in the process, the most quietly devotional installation in the Biennale this year.

more.title: Gideon Gomo: from stone to bronze, the ancestral algorithm at Venice
more.description: The fourth in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Gideon Gomo's first sustained body of bronze work, paired with discarded vehicle number plates and Zimbabwean springstone, is the move of a sculptor at full maturity rethinking the material vocabulary of his country's most internationally legible art form.

more.title: Shumba Maasai in Venice
more.description: At the 61st Biennale, the Zimbabwean artist's day-long procession through the city's campi, performed under the borrowed authority of a Murehwa chief, restages the social-sculpture lineage of Joseph Beuys for a contemporary African discursive practice.

more.title: Franklyn Dzingai: family albums and the neural commons at Venice
more.description: The third in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Franklyn Dzingai's reductive cardboard prints, built from the family album, the magazine clipping, the back-room studio photographs of mid-century Zimbabwe, turn out, on close looking, to be the most precisely engineered work in the room.

more.title: Felix Shumba: charcoal and the practice of disappearance at Venice
more.description: The second in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Felix Shumba's six-metre charcoal wall and his Telephone Reader series anchor a body of work that argues drawing is not the act of capturing the seen, but the act of staying present to what is on its way out of sight.

more.title: UK services share in Zimbabwe rises to 8.2%
more.description: The headline UK services trade with Zimbabwe softened year on year. The structural number underneath it tells the better story: Britain's share of all services Zimbabwe imports rose 2.4 percentage points to 8.2%, the highest measured level in years. As Zimbabwean buyers re-rate their global supplier mix, the UK is taking a bigger slice of the country's services demand.

more.title: Zimbabwe's demand lifts British exports by 14.6%
more.description: The Zimbabwean economy is in its strongest stretch in years: 7.5% GDP growth in 2025, a projected 5% in 2026, and that growth is pulling demand for cars, commercial vehicles, medicines and industrial machinery in with it. UK goods exports to Zimbabwe rose 14.6% on the year, the strongest line in an otherwise soft bilateral picture for Britain.

more.title: Victoria Falls anthem Kasambabezi captures audiences
more.description: Mthabisi Tshuma, mthabisimthire@gmail.com THE collaborative track Kasambabezi, a musical tribute to Victoria Falls, is receiving positive reviews across digital platforms while creating new opportunities for the local artistes behind the project. Produced by DJ Ace Kater, also known as Joseph Black, the song brings together emerging artistes, including Smokey P, Dos Pinky, Alka Tin, Madhuku, Uncle Bobos, Obvidoe, Maskal, Criss Ozzy, Muzie, Raaz, J Tonga and Blaq Spider. Each artiste delivers a unique verse, highlighting their talent while celebrating the heritage and beauty of Victoria Falls, one of the Seven Natural Wonders of the World. Project organiser Handsome Handy Sibanda said the team has been encouraged by the reception the project has received since its release. “The project brought together 14 artistes with the aim of celebrating our culture, showcasing the incredible talent that exists in our community and demonstrating what can be achieved when artists unite behind a common vision. “In a relatively short space of time, the music video has surpassed over 3 000 views on YouTube and what has been even more rewarding is the overwhelmingly positive feedback from viewers. The comments on YouTube, as well as the reactions across the individual artists' social media platforms, have been filled with praise for the quality of the production, the collaboration and the message behind the song, said Handy. He said the project's impact extends beyond the number of views it has accumulated. “While success is not measured by views alone, the engagement tells us that the project has resonated with people. It has sparked conversations, increased visibility for all the participating artistes and inspired many who have expressed a desire to see more collaborations of this nature. “We believe Kasambabezi is achieving its objectives. It has united artistes, promoted local talent and created a sense of pride within the community. Of course, this is just the beginning. We will continue promoting the song and exploring more opportunities to ensure it reaches an even wider audience both within Zimbabwe and beyond, he said. Beyond celebrating Victoria Falls, Kasambabezi demonstrates the power of collaboration, providing a platform for emerging artistes to showcase their abilities while positioning local talent for greater recognition on the national and international stage.

more.title: Homela’s 80th birthday wish: Save grassroots football
more.description: Lovemore Dube lovemore.dube@chronicle.co.zw VETERAN football coach and Zimbabwe Saints legend Gibson Homela has used his 80th birthday celebrations to make a passionate appeal for greater investment in grassroots football, the revival of his beloved Zimbabwe Saints and reforms to football development in Zimbabwe. Homela, who turned 80 on Wednesday, made the remarks during a birthday celebration organised by his children and attended by football personalities, community leaders and representatives from the corporate sector. At 80, I don't want gifts for myself. I want a promise for the next generation. A promise that we will take grassroots soccer seriously, said Homela. He also appealed for the revival of Zimbabwe Saints, the club where he made his name as both a player and coach. I want a promise that we will revive our legends like Zimbabwe Saints, said the Chikwata and Zimbabwe Saints legend. Homela said what Zimbabwean football needs now is action rather than words. Journalists, influencers, leaders - please help me carry this message. The best birthday gift you can give me is action. Let's build the future of Zimbabwean football, together, said Homela. Reflecting on the impact Zimbabwe Saints had on his life, Homela said the club had shaped not only his career but also the identity of many people in Bulawayo. The club gave me, and so many others, an identity. It gave our community Pride. Today Saints are fighting in the First Division, but they are fighting without enough support. I appeal to our business leaders, our corporates, and our Government to back Zimbabwe Saints with sponsorship. Give them the resources to succeed in the First Division. When the Saints rise, Bulawayo rises, said Homela. The veteran gaffer made his debut for Zimbabwe Saints, then known as Mashonaland FC, in 1963 after being identified at Gweru's Gafa Grounds alongside Aleck Mwanza and the legendary William Sibanda. He also challenged Government to introduce football coaching as part of the curriculum at teachers' colleges to strengthen grassroots development. Please introduce soccer as part of the curriculum in our teachers' colleges. Let every student teacher graduate not just as an educator, but as a qualified soccer according to FIFA and Caf standards, said Homela. He said investing in teachers was equivalent to investing in hundreds of children and would have a lasting impact on football development across the country. Homela described the football coaches who attended his birthday celebration as part of his legacy, saying the game had given him purpose and shaped his life. It taught me that our children are the future of this beautiful game. Right now our talent is being left behind. We find them in the streets, in dusty fields, with so much talent and so little structure, said Homela.
